posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
OK. Finally done. Because there was such a delay at my end, I bent my usual rule and allowed latecomers choices to be included. We ended up with 620 wines to consider.

It should come as no surprise that a Champagne rules the roost again. The 2008 Taittinger Comtes de Champagne is the winner with 4 mentions.

Four-way tie for 2nd with 3 votes each.

2008 Chateau Rayas
1982 Mouton-Rothschild
2010 Lopez De Heredia Vina Tondonia Rosado
1997 Ridge Monte Bello

In terms of house numbers, some of the usual suspects are in play. I will list all houses with 5 mentions or more

DRC – 15
Krug – 13
Taittinger – 12
Dom – 11
Lopez de Heredia – 9
D’Yquem – 8 (actually a little low for them historically)
Ridge – 8
JJ Prum – 7
Domaine Leflaive – 7 (nice comeback for them after a couple lean years here and there)
Rayas – 7
Coche-Dury – 7
Allemand – 6
Haut Brion – 6 (Ilkka had a lot more, lucky dog , but I chose to pare his list of those down a little for tabulation purposes)
Guigal – 6
Latour – 5
Margaux – 5
Clos Rougeard – 5
Huet – 5
Keller – 5
Trimbach – 5
Selosse – 5

Off the list entirely? Well, Bond had no mentions, nor did Maybach. No Marcassin at all. Just one Grange Hermitage (no HOG, but we rarely see notes on that). No Giacosa. At all. That was a surprise to me. Roulot just one mention and Ramonet just 2. No Egon Muller at all. And for Cristal? 4 mentions, 2 each for the 2002 and 2008 version. The other big surprise was only 3 mentions for Salon. But otherwise, a really spread-out list with a lot of producers I haven’t heard of before, which is great fun.

Thanks for playing again this year everyone! To a more in-person 2022, I hope.

Mike

posts.name: David Bueker

2021 has been a bit of a step back for me. Things got a little fatalistic in 2020, and I opened a lot of “great” wines. Some impressed, and some didn’t. 2021 went part way back to normal, and I dove deeper into “regular” wines, which also set up the beginnings of my next phase of wine, where I will be pulling back expenditures as I edge closer to retirement.

As far as wine of the year & discovery of the year, it’s the same wine. I was late to the game with the Champagnes of Benoit Dehu, but the 2015 Cuvée De L’Orme has caused me to dive in and catch up. My wife was also a big fan, and asked if I could get more. Needless to say I did!

wow, I used to be able to rely on memory for these thread but for some reason it’s gotten a lot harder in recent years.

Fortunately I have the Rougeard/Baudry dinner to fall back on. I need to type up my notes and add them to the thread but in the meantime here were my favorites for current drinking. All were fantastic:

2009 Baudry La Crois Boissee
2006 Clos Rougeard Poyeaux
2005 Clos Rougeard Poyeaux
1997 Baudry La Crois Boissee
1997 Clos Rougeard Clos


Other than that there was a 2004 Clos Roche Blanche Cabernet which miraculously survived its artificial cork to develop into a wine of enthralling beauty, an Etienne Calsac Revenants which stood out at a dinner for its vibrancy and complexity, and a 1982 La Dominique which has been coasting along on a plateau of delicious maturity for the last 20 years. I know I’ve forgotten others…

Adding the 1984 Laurel Glen Cabernet which was stellar at the PDH Berserkerfest.

Adding the 1855 Gonzalez Byass 1911 Coronation Sherry which was easily WOTY for me.


Discovery would be Mencia. I had my first one - a La Vizcaina (Raul Perez) 2018 Lomas de Valtuille “La Vitoriana” and am intrigued.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
As of now it is:

2010 Reynvaan Family Vineyards Syrah In the Rocks - USA, Washington, Columbia Valley, Walla Walla Valley (10/9/2021)
Day 1: Holy cow! This was the wine of the night at the last offline I went to prior to the Pandemic and this one is singing. Roasted meats, white pepper, blueberries, blackberries, Asian spices, mild funk and stone. Super concentrated wine and this rivals any Cayuse wine I have tasted. 95 points (95 points)

2015 Evesham Wood Chardonnay Mahonia Vineyard - USA, Oregon, Willamette Valley (6/28/2021)
Brother in Town Lot’s of Wines!!!; 6/26/2021-7/4/2021: Day 1: One if not the best Domestic Chardonnay I have ever tasted. This is high end Burgundy without the price point. Flint, spice, shells, orange peel, saline, tart apples and dust. Aged in neutral French Oak. Medium to long finish and amazing acidity. This is a must have in a blind White Burgundy tasting. 95 points
Day 2: Has not lost any complexity and maybe even more vibrant than day 1. For under $40.00 this is an amazing steal. 95+
Recommendation: Buy this immediately. I can’t believe they still have 20 cases of this wine left but my brother and I just took one of their hands. (95 points)

2014 Davenport Cellars Mr. B - USA, Washington, Columbia Valley (1/28/2021)
Day 1: Well there have been some amazing bottles but this one is off the charts. Cherry, dust, exotic spices, dark chocolate, cigar and crushed blueberries. Medium to long finish. Complexity off the charts. WOW! 95 points (95 points)

2019 DeLille Cellars Chaleur Estate Blanc - USA, Washington, Columbia Valley (10/8/2021)
Day 1: Stones, lemon rind, orange peel, spice, minerals and floral notes. Medium to long finish nice acidity. This is a back the truck up wine. 94 points
Day 2: Still vibrant. Amazing how good this is and not even two years in bottle. Floral, white peaches, lemon rind, orange peel, mild spice and minerals. What a refreshing wine on an 80 degree, October day in MN. 93 points
Day 3: Certainly not as good as day 1 or 2. Not surprised.
Recommendation: Drink now to 2028. This is an absolute Rockstar wine on sale for $23.99. (94 points)

2017 Bodegas Muga Rioja Reserva - Spain, La Rioja, La Rioja Alta, Rioja (10/2/2021)
Day 1: All all young Muga Reserva’s these improve over time. Earth, spices, floral notes, coffee beans, minerals and red currants. Tight wine that should open up over the next few days. 90-92 points
Day 2: Better. Nice concentration featuring roasted chestnuts, earth, spice, floral, cherry, sour raspberries and minerals. Medium plus finish and delicious. Great value at $25.00. 91 points
Day 3: Hoping for even more improvement and got it! This the real deal and for those who don’t like the wood/dill of some Rioja wines this one is for you! Raspberries, minerals, sour cherry, spice, mild wood note, roasted chestnuts and dried rose petals. Medium plus finish and complexity overload. Might be one of the better under $30 Spanish Wines in a long time. 93+ points
Day 4: A rare day 4 at 3 Day Wine Review headquarters. Amazing how good this is on day 4. Has not lost a step. 93 points
Recommendation: BUY IT! (93 points)

2014 Andrew Will Two Blondes Vineyard - USA, Washington, Columbia Valley, Yakima Valley (9/18/2021)
Day 1: Pop and pour. Black licorice, smoked meats, dark fruits, menthol and damp earth. A bit of heat on the nose a some cinnamon on the finish. Will be sampling this on day 1.5 to see if these blows over but still a tasty wine at this point with of course plenty of aging potential.
Day 1.5: Wow a two hour decant or half a day later this is rocking. Cassis, spice, graphite, menthol, stone and minerals. Complex effort and one of the better values for Washington State. 93+ points
Day 2: Not as good or even close. This needs to be consumed on day 1 or long term aging. Very chalky and not enjoyable. 82 points
Day 3: A split between day 1 and 2. Features sour cherries, minerals, dust and underbrush. Would be upset if this is the wine on day 1 but still drinkable. 88 points
Recommendation: This wine could be aged for 5 to 8 years although was really please on day 1 results. Decant for an hour or 2 and drink up on day 1. (93 points)

2013 Davenport Cellars Continuity - USA, Washington, Columbia Valley (7/22/2021)
Day 1: Last bottle. This is rich and impressive. Disregard the high alcohol on these wines as it does not show. Blueberries, spices, cherry, minerals and mint. Medium plus finish and nice acidity. Excellent wine. 92 points
Day 2: Very nice and flavorful featuring blueberries, spice, menthol, mint, cherry and dust. Medium plus finish. 92 points
Day 3: Maybe a tade bit lower but still holding strong. 91 points
Day 5: Well sometimes patients is rewarded and in this case yes it was! Blueberries, Bing cherries, menthol, dust, minerals, dark chocolate and gravel. Medium to long finish and concentration is intense. I would put this against any Napa Valley wine under $60. 94 points
Recommendation: There is no hurry in drinking the 13 Continuity. (94 points)

2013 Figgins Estate Red Wine - USA, Washington, Columbia Valley, Walla Walla Valley (6/28/2021)
Brother in Town Lot’s of Wines!!!; 6/26/2021-7/4/2021: This was amazing on just a pop and pour. Got better over the night however. Bing cherries, plum, spice, espresso, earth and subtle wood. Very balanced wine and amazing concentration. This should drink well for the next decade. Drink 2021 to 2031. (94 points)

2014 White Rock Vineyards Cabernet Sauvignon - USA, California, Napa Valley (6/3/2021)
Day 1: Wow! Bordeaux like funk along with cherry, minerals, tar, gravel and underbrush. Medium plus and concentrated finish. Wish every Napa Valley wine delivered at this price. 94 points
Day 2: Still showing that nice funk with cherry, plum, minerals, gravel and underbrush. Some nice spice on the finish. Very concentrated effort and a super solid wine for under $100. 94 points
Day 3: This wine is going to age through 2030. Beautiful on day 3. Gravel, mild funk, spice, blueberries, cherry, plum and bell peppers. Medium to long finish. One of the better Napa wines for under $100.00 I have tasted in the last decade. 94 points
Recommendation: Buy it! (94 points)

2018 Januik Syrah Ciel du Cheval Vineyard - USA, Washington, Columbia Valley, Red Mountain (4/30/2021)
Day 1: Now we are talking! This is awesome. Roasted meats, blueberries in the house, cedar dust, crushed rock and floral notes. This is on a pop and pour. Just think what this will do over time. Super long, concetrated finish. Love the Red Mountain. 94 points
Day 2: Solid but not the wow of day 1. Roasted meats, coffee beans, cedar, black pepper, smoke and dark fruits. Normally a Syrah this young should improve on day 2 or 3 but not this one. Pop and pour. 92 points (94 points)

So far…

1982 Mouton Rothschild- Drank along side with the other 4 First Growths for my 40th birthday (81’s took a backseat in favor of the legendary vintage) and this bottle was firing on all cylinders. One of the greatest Bordeaux’s I’ve ever had the pleasure of drinking. Latour was a close second but still super youthful and needed more time.

1999 Salon- Enjoyed at wine event in Atlanta with several members of this board. Absolutely Incredible bottle which far outshined the 04’ we had at the same time.

2001 Harlan Estate. Perfect in every way and top 3 Napa Cabs I’ve ever had. Perhaps the perfect pairing as well didn’t hurt it’s star. Drank from Magnum along side A5 Wagyu Tartare.

posts.name: jordan jacobs
posts.cooked:
1982 Mouton - had several but one was screaming

Just curious since I’ve heard so much about the unreliability of the '82 - how many bottles? And were the others just shy of screaming or terrible?

I had three this year. All were excellent. One was big tight but greatness still in the making. The second was much more open and everything an excellent mouton would be. The third was the most advanced but displayed all the great mouton secondary and Tertiary notes, delivered on ripe savory fruit. Tremendous complexity behind the green pepper notes gave some finesse to something very powerful. No abatement of fruit either. The others were easy 96+ point wines, the later was as close to perfection as I can see a wine being. Better than an 82 Latour I had earlier in the year. Better than then the best 82 Lalande I have ever had too.

Sancerre Vatan Clos la Néore 2007 – september 2020 - GD17,5 – LG17,5 (Report by Guillaume Deschepper)
Après Grillet, autre grande bouteille réussissant le tour de force, par-delà son caractère taiseux, de diffuser avec parcimonie l’exotisme naturel de son cépage (fleur de sureau, maracuja), pour mieux parler de sol et d’énergie. Cette réserve prend ici la forme d’une légère réduction organique (viande froide, rillette) et d’un voile aromatique coquillier, versant en bouche l’écho janséniste de puissantes saveurs vibrantes, calcaires et marines.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
2016 Powers Cabernet Sauvignon Reserve Champoux Vineyard - USA, Washington, Columbia Valley, Horse Heaven Hills (11/28/2021)
Day 1: Medium purple. Full bodied effort featuring blackberries, dark cherry, smoke, wood, spice box, minerals and rose petals. This is one of the better Cabernet Sauvignon’s I have tasted from Washington State for the money in a long time. $25.00 on sale, more please. 93 points
Day 2: Less intense than day 1 but equally delicious. Spices, cassis, smoke, cigar, minerals, earth and dark plums. Medium plus finish and really nice wine. Hard to find this quality of Cabernet anywhere for under $25.00. 93 points
Day 3: Still very good but consume this on day 1 or 2 for maximum enjoyment.
Recommendation: A stellar but for the money and better than many much more expensive Washington State Cabs. (93 points)

2004 Vilmart Coeur de Cuvee is (at least at this point) my WOTY, Grammy Grams. My discovery is simply how good the Laherte Freres NV Rose de Meunier is bottle after bottle.

You and I are usually pretty closely aligned, but after two bottles of the Laherte Rosé, we are 0 for 2. My wife didn’t like the first one, and we both disliked the second. We just found it monotone and almost bitter. We both usually love Meunier wines, so it was shocking and disappointing. Just so weird. I have a couple of other Laherte wines, and will bring them to dinners/tastings, so I can try them, but not end up struggling through them if they don’t work for me.

Bingo. Bought three bottles of the Laherte rose on this board’s high praise and found the first two at least to be one-dimensional and rather boring. I’ve enjoyed the BdB Brut Nature, though. We’ll see if the third bottle of rose shows differently but glad to see I’m not the only one who found it underwhelming.

DOTY
2019 Fattoria Moncucchetto Il Murchì Bianco - white from Ticino, Switzerland. The grape is Johanniter, which is a “research-bred” cross from Riesling. All of the wines from varieties created by research institutes (e.g., Cornell, UCDavis etc.) I have tried prior to the Johanniter have been “curiosities” (aka barely drinkable). This Johanniter was delicious and a good QPR (CHF 18); its Riesling ancestry is obvious on the nose and palate, though it is lighter-bodied.

posts.name: Ashish
posts.cooked:
My consumption this year is way down. Less entertaining, fewer opportunities to open a bottle. (Had I opened as many bottles as last year my cellar would have shrunk, but instead it continues to grow…)

Plenty of good wines though. Here are some highlights:

Sparkles
1996 Krug - Delicious, representative of the Krug style, incredibly complex. But also ready now and showing full maturity, and I don’t see any reason to age this further. I’m not a fan of super ancient champagne though… 25 years on this was more than enough for me.

Whites
1977 Bonneau du Martray Corton Charlemagne - Opened as a bit of an experiment, but this was a standout with some really exceptional wines.
2019 Falkenstein Auslese #5 (Forster) - I know the drier wines get all the hype, but this auslese was fantastic. Incredibly well balanced, and really not that sweet.

Red
G. Rinaldi 2011 Tre Tine - First tasted on my honeymoon where it launched a fascination with Barolo, this subsequent bottle held up to that initial experience. Drinking beautifully now.
2007 Allemand Reynard - This was an expensive bottle for me, because I went out after and bought some recent vintage Allemand. And I know that compared to other vintages of Allemand this probably was middle of the pack.

Sauternes
2001 Suduiraut - Always a beauty, still showing superbly.
2001 Fargues - Always one of my favorites too.

Heitz Martha’s Vineyard 1974

The Ridge was brought to our Steven Spurrier memorial dinner during the Southwold tasting and the Heitz was the star of a dinner featuring mature Trotanoy, Petrus, Margaux and Lafite.

Greatest white / fizz would be a batch of Krug GC from the late 1980s and discovery is old NV Champagne: I bought some Bollinger Special Cuvee from the 1970s that was sensational and only about £80 a bottle.

posts.name: Tomás Costa
posts.cooked:
Gold medal: 1934 Kopke Colheita
Silver medal: 1964 Barca Velha
Bronze medal: 2019 Azores Wine Company Vinha dos Utras
Discoveries: Anything out of Textura Wines (Dão), new wines from Quinta da Alameda by Anselmo Mendes, Listrão wines from Porto Santo Island by António Maçanita.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
Discovery - 2007 Pegau Cuvee Reserve, even with a 3 hour decant, is no where near ready and probably has at least 10 years to reach its peak, but it’s got a lot going on at the back of the palate, with deep, dark berry fruit focused on raspberry and a strong spicy roasted backbone.

Man there are a lot of notes on this wine on tracker. Had it in 2012 and no where near ready. I would agree more time for sure.

posts.name: Marek Hnatyk
posts.cooked:
WOTY: Dunn Vineyards, Cabernet Sauvignon 2004, Howell Mountain - at peak now ( 96/100)
Discovery Of The Year: La Jota Cabernet Franc 1997 Howell Mountain - bought at 42 Euro, this particular bottle was superb ( 95/100), it is testimony of legendary period for Napa 1990-1997, made by ( in my opinion) the best winemaker of all time Helen Turley. Even R.Parker considered La Jota Cabernet Franc as best Cabernet Franc in North America in 90’s, it’s hard to disagree.

posts.name: andrew messenger
posts.cooked:
1961 Latour…cork floating in the bottle, foil keeping wine in. Fantastic

Petrus, no label!. Cork perfect, wine amazing. Friends wife who had no idea what it was said it was the best wine she ever had. I guess the wine to be from the 1975-1985 range based on other bottles in the collection.

2008 Screaming Eagle… my first ever. Etherial balance.

posts.name: BILL D A V I E S
posts.cooked:
Narutotai Ginjo Nama Genshu: Drunken Snapper Sake
Wakatake Onikoroshi-Demon Slayer Junmai Daiginjo

I know… not wines, but impressed me more than any wine has this year.

I think the wine we are selling most of now other than champagne is Pedroncelli Pinot Noir Signature Select. I tell customers it’s the pinot noir for people who hate pinot noir. It’s the opposite of delicate. Fairly rich in tannins and flavor and does not seem like pinot to me at all, but people love it.

I think in general nicer prosecco’s were my discovery this year like Adami.

Hi Mike,

I finally saw this thread and then had to do some memory searching before reviewing numerous notes to come up with a list and I know I`m leaving out many, so this is a “short” list:

1970 Gaja Barbaresco
1974 Gaja Barbaresco

1999 Araujo
2008 Laurent-Perrier
1964 Fontanafredda Barolo

My discovery of the year is that I have finally learned to cave in and quit taking notes on every wine I taste or when the total number in an evening goes beyond a few, like 10 or 12. I know, it`s supposed to be about a wine discovery, and there is one that stands out: Project M in Oregon. I was asked to review some of their wines and after 11 in all, 9 Pinots and 2 Rieslings, it was obvious, this is a great new kid on the block, so much so, I order a few 4 packs for myself and others for the holidays.

Cheers,
Blake
